evidence_quotes.quote: through pricing, bundling and other means, rightholders can restrict access to specific digital content that libraries may seek to acquire/access on behalf of their users
terms which would be considered as unfair if they were offered to individual consumers.
Explicit extension of the beneficiaries of protections against unfair digital contract terms to research and education institutions, on behalf of their users.
Guarantee that contractual terms cannot waive or nullify EU copyright exceptions, particularly for preservation, education, and Text and Data Mining.

paragraphs.text: This feedback is provided by the International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions, in its capacity as administrator of the Knowledge Rights 21 programme. This seeks to ensure that EU and national law guarantees the rights to research, education and culture in a digital age. Our contribution focuses in particular on problems with digital contracts. The EUs current approach to consumer protection has a fundamental flaw it does not provide any support for people who rely on access to libraries or other public institutions in order to be able to pursue research, learning, or fulfil their cultural rights. This is despite the fact that libraries and so their users face very similar challenges to individual consumers, and that relevant digital content markets have developed in ways that mean that there is no meaningful offer directly to consumers. Further details are provided in the attached contribution, which illustrates in more depth the similarities between typical terms offered to libraries, and terms which would be considered as unfair if they were offered to individual consumers. In short, we believe that a key contribution towards the Digital Fairness Acts objective of making digital content markets work better for individual Europeans would be to act to level the playing field around digital contracts for libraries and their host institutions. Particular challenges faced include: A) Constructive refusals to license: through pricing, bundling and other means, rightholders can restrict access to specific digital content that libraries may seek to acquire/access on behalf of their users B) Lack of guarantee around service offered: libraries have little visibility around which items will be added to or taken from a bundle of subscribed content C) Imposition of disproportionate liability: libraries are asked to take on extensive liability for the actions of their users, despite ensuring adequate safeguards D) Override of rights: digital contracts often backed up by technological protection measures undermine the rights created by exceptions and limitations to copyright, either directly or through using choice-of-law principles to opt out of EU law E) Libraries can no longer fulfil their social mission to preserve content for future generations, given that they often no longer own content As a result, we recommend: 1) Explicit extension of the beneficiaries of protections against unfair digital contract terms to research and education institutions, on behalf of their users. 2) Adaptation of the contract terms classified as unfair to ensure that they also apply to the type of situations faced by libraries and other research and education institutions. 3) Create a right of access on reasonable (FRAND) terms to digital content for libraries, including the right of permanent access for preservation purposes. 4) Clarify that once a reasonable period of time has passed from a request (72h or three working days), libraries and institutions can use all relevant means to remove or circumvent technological protection measures which prevent the use of copyright material in line with copyright exceptions and limitations. Further structural steps will help ensure fairer practices when it comes to digital content markets: 5) Establish and protect a right to digital ownership for libraries and consumers, so that any digital output made available to the public must also be offered for sale with full ownership rights, free from perpetual technological or contractual restrictions. 6) Explicitly recognise and protect library lending of owned digital copies, such as through enabling the digitisation of physical books and lending of subsequent digital copies. 7) Guarantee that contractual terms cannot waive or nullify EU copyright exceptions, particularly for preservation, education, and Text and Data Mining.
